synthesis_text: Start by throttling login traffic: introduce rate limiting or exponential back‑off on the login endpoint (e.g., per‑IP/IP‑range limits) to cut the flood of failed attempts, which immediately eases DB lock contention. Next, add or tune indexes on the login‑related columns (username, email, etc.) to shorten the duration of SELECT/UPDATE statements and reduce lock time. If contention persists, offload authentication reads to a read‑replica or cache (Redis) and consider shortening transaction critical sections. Strengthen security further with temporary account lockout or CAPTCHA after repeated failures. Finally, monitor DB lock metrics and login‑failure rates after each change to validate stability and security improvements.

Start by cutting the lock time that drives latency: analyze the login‑related transactions and shrink their critical sections (N1:0:aa8cb934), then add or tune indexes on the columns used in authentication queries (N1:1:1235ed7f).  If the workload still holds locks, switch the authentication queries to READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT or a lower isolation level (N2:1:a488b1a3) and consider offloading read‑only authentication checks to a replica or cache such as Redis (N2:2:f02ba297).  Once the database contention is mitigated, harden security and further reduce load by rate‑limiting login attempts per IP or user (N3:0:3f54a1bd, N5:0:e17ec966) and applying temporary account lockout after repeated failures (N5:2:7b9154c6).  Monitor lock metrics and failure rates after each change to validate improvements.

Implement rate limiting (or IP throttling) on the login endpoint as the first change. By capping the number of login attempts per IP or per user, you immediately curb brute‑force traffic, lower the burst of authentication requests, and thus reduce DB lock contention and latency. After deploying rate limiting, monitor DB lock metrics and login‑failure rates to confirm the stability and security gains before adding further optimizations such as indexing or account lockout.

Start by adding rate limiting (or exponential back‑off) on the login endpoint to throttle the flood of failed attempts; this immediately cuts the number of concurrent authentication queries, easing DB lock contention and mitigating brute‑force attacks. Next, add indexes on the login‑related columns (e.g., username, email) to speed up SELECT/UPDATE operations, which shortens the time each transaction holds locks. Reduce the duration of the critical sections in the authentication transactions to further lower lock time. As a follow‑up security hardening step, introduce CAPTCHA or temporary account lockout after repeated failures to block automated attacks.

First, introduce rate‑limiting (or exponential back‑off) on the login endpoint to throttle repeated failed attempts; this immediately cuts the brute‑force traffic that is inflating DB write load and improves security. Next, shorten the duration of login‑related DB transactions to reduce lock hold time, and add indexes on the columns used in login queries (e.g., username, email) to speed up SELECT/UPDATE operations, further lowering contention. Finally, monitor lock and failure metrics to verify the impact of these changes.
